1: dei perissoteros prosechein hēmas tois akoustheisin, mēpote pararrūomen — "we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it" (pararrūomen: to drift past, to flow past — a nautical term for a ship drifting past its harbor because the pilot failed to steer properly): the image of passive drifting is the warning against neglect rather than active apostasy. The comparative perissoteros (more exceedingly) grounds the increased attentiveness requirement in the increased magnitude of the new covenant revelation.
3: pōs hēmeis ekpheuxometha tēlikaytēs amelēsantes sōtērias — "how shall we escape if we neglect such a great salvation?" (amelēsantes: having neglected, having cared nothing for — aorist participle): amelēo (to be unconcerned about, to neglect) implies passive indifference rather than active rejection — the warning is against those who simply do not take salvation seriously. The tēlikautēs (so great) grounds the impossibility of escape: the magnitude of the salvation corresponds to the magnitude of the consequence for neglecting it.
9: ton de brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon blepomen Iēsoun dia to pathēma tou thanatou doxē kai timē estephanōmenon — "but we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death" (brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōmenon: made lower for a little — the Ps 8 phrase applied to the incarnate humiliation): the name Iēsoun (Jesus) appears here for the first time in Hebrews, positioned after the description of humiliation — the human name of the one who took the human condition. Doxē kai timē estephanōmenon (crowned with glory and honor) — the perfect passive participle indicates the permanent crown of the exaltation state.
10: eprepen gar autō... ton archēgon tēs sōtērias autōn dia pathēmatōn teleiōsai — "it was fitting for him... to make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering" (archēgon: pioneer, captain, founder — one who leads the way, the first to go through a path that others will follow; teleiōsai: to perfect, complete, bring to the goal — the teleio- root pervades Hebrews): archēgos is not merely an author who initiates from a distance but a pioneer who personally traverses the route. The perfection through suffering is not moral improvement (the Son was not morally deficient) but the completion of the vocational path — the suffering was necessary for the high-priestly qualification.
11: ho te gar hagiazōn kai hoi hagiazomenoi ex henos pantes — "for he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source" (ex henos pantes: all from one — the henos is either masculine [one father, i.e., Abraham, or God] or neuter [one origin/source]): the shared origin establishes the solidarity that makes the Son's brotherhood genuine rather than merely analogical. Di' hēn aitian ouk epaischynetai adelphous autous kalein — "for which reason he is not ashamed to call them brothers" (ouk epaischynetai: is not ashamed — the social-shame context: the exalted one acknowledging his solidarity with the lowly).
17: hothen ōpheilen kata panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai, hina eleēmōn genētai kai pistos archiereus ta pros ton theon eis to hilaskesthai tas hamartias tou laou — "therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest in the service of God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people" (hilaskesthai: to propitiate, make atonement — the LXX verb for the priestly atonement act on the Day of Atonement [Lev 16:20 LXX]; used for covering/averting divine wrath against sin): the hilaskesthai debate centers on whether "expiation" (neutralizing sin's pollution) or "propitiation" (averting God's wrath) is primary. The LXX usage and the wrath-context of Hebrews favor propitiation, though the two are not sharply separable. Eleēmōn kai pistos (merciful and faithful) — the two qualifications for a legitimate high priest who genuinely represents the people before God.
18: en hō gar peponthen autos peirastheis, dynatai tois peirazomenois boēthēsai — "for because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted" (peponthen: perfect passive of paschō = to suffer; peirastheis: having been tested/tempted — aorist passive participle): the perfect tense of peponthen (he has suffered, with the suffering-effect remaining) grounds the present capacity to help. The suffering is not merely past but bears on his present qualification. Boēthēsai (to come to the aid, to help — from boaō = to shout + theō = to run): to run to the cry of one who calls for help — the helping is urgent and responsive.
2:
ei gar ho di' angelōn lalētheis logos egeneto bebaios — "For if the message declared through angels was reliable" (bebaios: firm, reliable, legally valid — a legal-commercial term for a guarantee that stands; used in papyri for a deed that conveys secure ownership): the Torah was understood in Second Temple tradition to have been mediated by angels (Deut 33:2 LXX; Gal 3:19; Acts 7:53) — its reliability (bebaios) is the argumentative baseline. If angel-mediated law carried penal force, how much more does Son-mediated salvation demand a response.

kai pasa parabasis kai parakoē elaben endikon misthapodosian — "and every transgression and act of disobedience received a just penalty" (parabasis: transgression, stepping over a boundary [para + bainō]; parakoē: disobedience, failure to hear fully [para + akouō]): the two terms cover active violation of explicit command (parabasis) and passive failure to heed (parakoē). Endikos misthapodosian (just reward/penalty — misthapodosia: payment of wages due): the penalty is not arbitrary but proportional and legally due.
4:
synepimartyroutos theou sēmeiois te kai terasin kai poikilais dynamesin kai pneumatos hagiou merismoiis kata tēn autou thelēsin — "while God confirmed their message with signs, wonders, various miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will" (synepimartyroutos: co-testifying with, bearing joint-witness — syn + epi + martyreō: an intensified compound; God's confirmatory witness accompanied the apostolic message): the three nouns sēmeia (signs), terata (wonders), dynameis (acts of power, miracles) are a stock NT formulaic sequence for the miraculous attestation of divine activity.

pneumatos hagiou merismoiis kata tēn autou thelēsin — "gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to his will" (merismoiis: distributions, dividings-out — from merizō; merismos implies sovereign apportionment): the Spirit-gifts are not human attainments but divine distributions allocated according to God's own will (kata tēn autou thelēsin), not the recipient's worthiness or request.
5: ou gar angelois hypetaxen tēn oikoumenēn tēn mellousan, peri hēs laloumen — "For God did not subject the coming world — the world we are speaking about — to angels" (oikoumenēn tēn mellousan: the coming inhabited world — oikoumenē is the inhabited earth/Roman world, but here in its eschatological sense, the world-to-come): the parenthetical peri hēs laloumen (about which we speak) identifies the topic: the coming world is the realm under discussion throughout. Angels govern the present age (understood in Second Temple cosmology); the coming age will not be theirs.
6: diemartyrato de pou tis legōn, Ti estin anthrōpos hoti mimnēskē autou, ē huios anthrōpou hoti episkeptē auton — "But there is a place where someone has testified: What is man that you are mindful of him, or the son of man that you care for him?" (Ps 8:4 LXX): the indefinite attribution (diemartyrato de pou tis: testified somewhere someone) is a deliberate distancing device — Hebrews cites the Psalm's divine authority, not the human author's identity. Ti estin anthrōpos (What is man?) — the Psalm's original frame is human smallness contrasted with God's grandeur. Hebrews will read it as a question whose only adequate answer is the Son of Man who became low and was exalted. Episkeptē (you care for/visit — from episkeptomai: to look upon with care, to visit with saving intent; the word carries the covenantal visit-to-rescue connotation).
7: ēlatōsas auton brachy ti par' angelous, doxē kai timē estephanōsas auton, kai katestēsas auton epi ta erga tōn cheirōn sou — "You made him a little lower than the angels; you crowned him with glory and honor and set him over the works of your hands" (Ps 8:5-6 LXX): brachy ti par' angelous ēlatōsas (you made lower by a little than/beside angels — ēlatōsas: aorist of elattoō, to diminish, lower; brachy ti: a little, for a short time): Hebrews will read the "little" as both degree (slightly lower in rank) and duration (for a brief time of humiliation). The LXX reads the Hebrew elohim as "angels" — Hebrews follows the LXX. Estephanōsas (you crowned — aorist): the crowning is the exaltation-event, historically completed.
8: panta hypetaxas hypokatō tōn podōn autou... nyn de oupō horōmen autō ta panta hypotetagmena — "You have put everything under his feet... yet at present we do not see everything subject to him": panta hypetaxas hypokatō tōn podōn autou (Ps 8:6b LXX) — "everything" is the universal scope, including the angels. En tō gar hypotaxai autō ta panta, ouden aphēken autō anypotakton (in the subjecting of all things to him, he left nothing not subjected to him) — the grammar is deliberate: the aorist subjection (hypotaxai) is comprehensive in scope but the present experience does not yet match it. Nyn de oupō horōmen (but now not yet we see) — the "not yet" is eschatological, not an admission of failure; the tension between "all things subjected" and "not yet visible" is resolved in v9 by pointing to the one who already went through death and was crowned.
12: legōn, Apaggeleō to onoma sou tois adelphois mou, en mesō ekklēsias hymnēsō se — "saying: I will proclaim your name to my brothers and sisters; in the midst of the assembly I will sing your praise" (Ps 22:22 LXX): the citation puts Ps 22 in the mouth of the Son — the Psalm that begins with the cry of dereliction (Ps 22:1, quoted at the cross) ends with the resolution to proclaim God's name to the community. Apaggeleō (I will report/announce — a strong public proclamation); en mesō ekklēsias (in the midst of the assembly/church — ekklēsia): the word the NT uses for the gathered community appears here in an OT psalm, read as the Son's own declaration of solidarity and praise.
13:
kai palin, Egō esomai pepoithōs ep' autō — "And again, I will trust in him" (Isa 8:17 LXX): a second OT citation placed in the Son's mouth. Pepoithōs ep' autō (trusting upon him — perfect participle of peithō: to convince; perfect = standing in a state of settled trust): the Son himself models the faith-posture to which Hebrews calls its community — trust in God even through suffering and apparent divine hiddenness, which is exactly the Isaiah 8 context (God hiding his face while the remnant trusts).

kai palin, Idou egō kai ta paidia ha moi edōken ho theos — "and again, Here am I and the children God has given me" (Isa 8:18 LXX): paidia (children — G3813, a term of smallness and dependence): the Son presents "the children" as those entrusted to him — the same solidary language as v11 (adelphous) and v14 (ta paidia). The double Isa 8 citation establishes the Son as the Servant-remnant figure who trusts God and maintains solidarity with his people through the dark time.
14:
epei oun ta paidia kekoinōnēken haimatos kai sarkos kai autos paraplēsiōs metesche tōn autōn — "Since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise took part in these" (kekoinōnēken: perfect active — they have and continue to share in flesh and blood, an established condition; metesche: aorist — he took part, a historical once-for-all act): the contrast of tenses is precise — the children's flesh-and-blood existence is their ongoing state; the Son's taking part is the historical incarnation event. Paraplēsiōs (in like manner, closely parallel — a comparative adverb expressing real resemblance, not mere analogy).

hina dia tou thanatou katargēsē ton to kratos echonta tou thanatou, tout' estin ton diabolon — "so that through death he might destroy the one who holds the power of death — that is, the devil" (katargēsē: aorist subjunctive of katargeō: to render inoperative, to nullify, to bring to nothing — not annihilation but de-powering): the means of destruction is the weapon itself — death is used against the death-holder. Ton to kratos echonta tou thanatou (the one having the power/strength of death): kratos (G2904) is brute power/dominion — the devil's grip on death is expressed as a possession he holds, not an inherent attribute.
15: kai apallaxē toutous, hosoi phobō thanatou dia pantos tou zēn enochoi ēsan douleias — "and free all those who all their lives had been held in slavery by their fear of death" (apallaxē: aorist subjunctive of apallassō: to release, set free, rid of — medical use for relieving a patient of disease): phobō thanatou (fear of death) — phobos here is the enslaving dread that structures human life under the threat of mortality. Enochoi ēsan douleias (were held liable to/subject to bondage): enochos (G1777) is a legal term — one who is bound/guilty; the slavery is legally constituted by the liability that the fear of death imposes on human existence.
16: ou gar dēpou angelōn epilambanetai, alla spermatos Abraam epilambanetai — "For surely it is not angels he helps, but Abraham's descendants" (epilambanetai: to take hold of, to seize, to grasp — present middle; the verb is vivid: the Son decisively lays hold of Abraham's seed): ou gar dēpou angelōn (for surely not of angels — dēpou is an emphatic particle asserting what is obvious or certain): the Son's assumption of human nature was directed specifically at Abraham's seed. Spermatos Abraam (Abraham's seed — sperma): the corporate seed of Abraham, the covenant people, is the specific humanity the Son joined — not human nature in general, and not angels at all.
